my_sentence: I had a good day today, stay at home, so I have enough time to do other things.
correction: I had a good day today. I stayed at home, so I had enough time to do other things.
explanation: A completed day is described with past-tense verbs. The teacher recast "have" as "had"; using "stayed" in the same past narrative is an assistant note.
correct_examples: I stayed at home, so I had time to read.
She had a quiet day yesterday.
context: Describing his day at home.

my_sentence: Having a good atmosphere can help you focus on your study.
correction: Having a good atmosphere can help you focus on your studies.
explanation: Use the plural "studies" when referring generally to someone's academic work. The teacher immediately supplied the plural form.
correct_examples: A quiet room helps me focus on my studies.
She takes her studies seriously.
context: Benefits of a good atmosphere in a library.

my_sentence: If I stayed there, I can feel calm.
correction: If I stay there, I can calm down.
explanation: The teacher supplied the phrasal verb "calm down." In this real present condition, use present tense in the if-clause; that tense repair is an assistant note.
correct_examples: If I stay in the museum, I can calm down.
Reading helps me calm down.
context: Describing the atmosphere of a science and technology museum.

my_sentence: I can see many magic things there.
correction: I can see many magical things there.
explanation: Use the adjective "magical" to describe things. The teacher prompted Jack to replace "magic" with "magical."
correct_examples: The museum has many magical exhibits.
It was a magical experience.
context: Talking about exhibits in a science and technology museum.

my_sentence: I can focus on their product and achievement.
correction: I can focus on their products and achievements.
explanation: Use plural countable nouns when referring generally to the museum's multiple products and achievements. The teacher explicitly supplied both plurals.
correct_examples: The exhibition displays new products and achievements.
We discussed their products.
context: Explaining what he observes in the museum.

my_sentence: Smartphone have changed from 100 years ago.
correction: Smartphones have changed since 100 years ago.
explanation: A general statement about this kind of device needs the plural "smartphones." The teacher repeatedly prompted the plural form.
correct_examples: Smartphones have changed rapidly.
Cars have become safer.
context: Giving smartphones as an example of changing technology.

my_sentence: Smartphones have changed from 100 years ago for now.
correction: Smartphones have changed from 100 years ago to now.
explanation: Use the paired prepositions "from ... to ..." to mark the beginning and end of a period. The teacher prompted "to."
correct_examples: Technology changed from then to now.
The shop is open from nine to five.
context: Comparing old and modern phones.

my_sentence: One of my coworker needs to go to different places.
correction: One of my coworkers needs to go to different places.
explanation: After "one of my," use a plural noun because the person is one member of a larger group. The teacher explicitly corrected "coworker" to "coworkers."
correct_examples: One of my coworkers travels every month.
One of her friends lives abroad.
context: Comparing his travel frequency with a coworker's.

my_sentence: One of my coworkers need to go to different places.
correction: One of my coworkers needs to go to different places.
explanation: The subject is singular "one," so the present-tense verb must be "needs." The teacher repeatedly prompted the final -s.
correct_examples: One of my coworkers needs to travel.
One of these machines works well.
context: Describing a project manager's monthly travel.

my_sentence: He is a employee just like me.
correction: He is an employee just like me.
explanation: Use "an" before a singular countable noun that begins with a vowel sound. The teacher explicitly recast the sentence.
correct_examples: He is an employee.
She is an engineer.
context: Clarifying that his coworker is not a businessman.

my_sentence: I can see this.
correction: I can see it.
explanation: Use the object pronoun "it" for the picture already being discussed. The teacher immediately supplied the corrected sentence.
correct_examples: I can see it clearly.
Please show it to me.
context: Confirming that the shared picture was visible.

my_sentence: There are some bad points of doing the job, such as too many business trips and raising families.
correction: There are some bad points to doing the job, such as too many business trips and raising the kids.
explanation: In this context, the specific responsibility is raising the children, so "raising the kids" is clearer than "raising families." The teacher explicitly corrected the phrase.
correct_examples: Raising the kids takes time.
Business trips make raising children harder.
context: Writing about the disadvantages of being a pilot.

my_sentence: You only can go home once or twice a week.
correction: You can only go home once or twice a week.
explanation: Place "only" after the modal verb "can" and before the main verb phrase. The teacher recast the sentence in that order.
correct_examples: I can only visit once a month.
She can only work on Fridays.
context: Explaining how often a pilot can return home.

my_sentence: Some of them are work hard and others do not.
correction: Some of them work hard and others do not.
explanation: Do not put "are" before the base verb in the simple present. The teacher explicitly removed it.
correct_examples: Some employees work hard.
Some people exercise and others do not.
context: Discussing whether Japanese workers are hardworking.

my_sentence: Some of my coworker like to stay game center to play all day.
correction: Some of my coworkers like to stay at the game center and play all day.
explanation: After "some of my," use the plural "coworkers." A place also needs the preposition and article "at the game center." The teacher explicitly supplied that place phrase.
correct_examples: Some of my coworkers play games after work.
They stay at the game center late.
context: Describing coworkers who play games at night.

my_sentence: You will find they are sleeping on their desk.
correction: You will find them sleeping on their desks. / You will find that they are sleeping on their desks.
explanation: Use "find + object + -ing" or a full that-clause. The teacher supplied both patterns.
correct_examples: You may find them sleeping at work.
You will find that they are resting.
context: Finding tired coworkers asleep in the office.

my_sentence: They are sleeping on their desk.
correction: They are sleeping on their desks.
explanation: With several people, each person's desk is naturally expressed with the plural "their desks." The teacher explicitly prompted the plural.
correct_examples: The employees are sitting at their desks.
The students left their bags on their desks.
context: Describing coworkers sleeping in the office.

my_sentence: You need to wake up them.
correction: You need to wake them up.
explanation: With a separable phrasal verb and a pronoun object, put the pronoun between the verb and particle: "wake them up."
correct_examples: Please wake me up at seven.
She woke the children up early.
context: Needing help from coworkers who are asleep.

my_sentence: They can't save any of money.
correction: They can't save enough money.
explanation: Use "enough" before the uncountable noun "money" to mean the amount is insufficient. The teacher prompted "enough."
correct_examples: They cannot save enough money.
We have enough time.
context: Discussing coworkers who spend all their income.

my_sentence: During that time my salary is not high. I only have 100,000 yuan.
correction: At that time, my salary was not high. I only had 100,000 yuan.
explanation: A past financial situation needs the past forms "was" and "had." The teacher corrected both verbs explicitly.
correct_examples: At that time, my salary was low.
I only had a small amount of savings.
context: Remembering his finances when he started a family.

my_sentence: You can't use your money buy a house or a car.
correction: You can't use your money to buy a house or a car.
explanation: Use the infinitive "to buy" to express the purpose for which money is used. The teacher supplied "to buy."
correct_examples: I used my savings to buy a car.
They used the loan to buy a house.
context: Explaining what his past savings could purchase.

my_sentence: Each company needed to pay higher taxes for them.
correction: Each company would need to pay a higher tax on them.
explanation: A single additional charge needs the article "a": "a higher tax." The teacher explicitly corrected the noun phrase.
correct_examples: The importer must pay a higher tax.
The government placed a tax on imported cars.
context: Discussing taxes on cars imported into the Philippines.

my_sentence: You can consider develop the country first.
correction: You can consider developed countries first.
explanation: Here "developed" is an adjective describing countries; "develop" is a verb. Jack repaired the phrase after the teacher asked for clarification.
correct_examples: You can consider developed countries first.
She is considering several countries.
context: Suggesting countries where the teacher could work.

my_sentence: There are many skills and knowledge that I don't learn.
correction: There are many skills and areas of knowledge that I haven't learned.
explanation: Use the present perfect for learning that has not happened up to now. The teacher explicitly supplied "have not learned."
correct_examples: There are many skills I haven't learned.
I haven't learned enough about business yet.
context: Explaining why he is not good at business.

my_sentence: I need a long time grow.
correction: I need a long time to grow.
explanation: Use the infinitive "to grow" after the noun phrase "a long time." The teacher repeatedly prompted the missing "to."
correct_examples: I need time to grow.
She needs a year to develop her skills.
context: Describing the time needed to develop business skills.

my_sentence: When you have a kid, you need to spend some time to play.
correction: When you have a kid, you need to spend some time with them.
explanation: Use "spend time with someone" when describing being together with that person. The teacher explicitly supplied the phrase.
correct_examples: Parents should spend time with their children.
I spent the afternoon with my daughter.
context: Explaining the time required to raise children.

my_sentence: If you have too much business trip, you will miss a lot.
correction: If you have too many business trips, you will miss a lot.
explanation: "Business trip" is countable, so use "too many" and the plural noun "business trips." The teacher corrected both forms explicitly.
correct_examples: Too many business trips can affect a family.
She has too many meetings this week.
context: Explaining how travel affects parenting.

my_sentence: For me, it's not interesting.
correction: For me, it wasn't interesting.
explanation: The activity had already finished, so use the past form "wasn't." The teacher explicitly prompted the past tense.
correct_examples: For me, it wasn't interesting.
The class wasn't difficult.
context: Giving an opinion about a past family activity.

my_sentence: It's not a boring.
correction: It wasn't boring.
explanation: "Boring" is an adjective, so do not put the article "a" before it. The past event also requires "wasn't," as the teacher supplied.
correct_examples: It wasn't boring.
The activity was fun.
context: Clarifying his opinion of the family activity.

my_sentence: Her mom sent her to school most times.
correction: Her mom sends her to school most of the time.
explanation: A regular present habit with the singular subject "her mom" takes "sends." The teacher explicitly prompted that form.
correct_examples: Her mom sends her to school.
My wife takes our daughter to class.
context: Describing who normally takes his daughter to school.

my_sentence: We played basketball, ran, and did other others.
correction: We played basketball, ran, and did other things.
explanation: Use "other" as a determiner before the plural noun "things." The teacher supplied the complete phrase "other things."
correct_examples: We played games and did other things.
She bought books and other items.
context: Listing games from a family activity.

my_sentence: I'd like to be a chief.
correction: I'd like to be a chef.
explanation: "Chef" /ʃef/ means a professional cook, while "chief" /tʃiːf/ means a leader. The teacher corrected the pronunciation in the complete sentence.
correct_examples: I'd like to be a chef.
The chef prepared dinner.
context: Naming a job he would like to have.

my_sentence: I'd dislike working long hours.
correction: I'd dislike working for long hours.
explanation: The teacher asked Jack to use "for" before the duration phrase and repeat the full sentence.
correct_examples: I'd dislike working for long hours.
She studied for three hours.
context: Naming a disadvantage of being a chef.

my_sentence: That will be fine for me.
correction: That would be fun for me.
explanation: Use "fun" for an enjoyable activity. "Fine" means acceptable or satisfactory. The teacher explicitly recast the sentence.
correct_examples: Teaching would be fun for me.
The schedule is fine for me.
context: Explaining why he might enjoy teaching.

my_sentence: There are some bad points, such as lower salary.
correction: There are some bad points, such as a low salary.
explanation: Use "low salary" for the general amount of pay. "Lower" needs an explicit or understood comparison. The teacher corrected it to "low salary."
correct_examples: One disadvantage is a low salary.
This job has a lower salary than that one.
context: Naming disadvantages of being a teacher.

my_sentence: I searched many places. I find Japan and the UK can provide a higher salary.
correction: I searched many places. I found that Japan and the UK can provide higher salaries.
explanation: Keep a completed search and its result in the past by using "found." The teacher explicitly prompted the past form.
correct_examples: I searched online and found several jobs.
We found that the cost was lower.
context: Researching countries with better salaries.

my_sentence: Japan and the UK can provide a higher salary.
correction: Japan and the UK are suitable for providing higher salaries.
explanation: The teacher recast the evaluation with "are suitable for" followed by the gerund "providing."
correct_examples: Those countries are suitable for building a career.
The role provides a higher salary.
context: Comparing possible countries for work.

my_sentence: You will find everything is so cheap.
correction: You will find that everything is so cheap.
explanation: Use "that" to introduce the complete clause that functions as the object of "find." The teacher repeatedly supplied the that-clause.
correct_examples: You will find that food is cheaper there.
I found that the rent was reasonable.
context: Describing prices when traveling abroad.

my_sentence: I can't speak Germany.
correction: I can't speak German.
explanation: "Germany" is the country; "German" is the language. The teacher explicitly supplied the language name.
correct_examples: I can't speak German.
She moved to Germany.
context: Explaining why moving to Germany would be difficult.

my_sentence: Sometimes you have to listen some German.
correction: Sometimes you have to listen to some German.
explanation: The verb "listen" requires the preposition "to" before its object. The teacher explicitly prompted it.
correct_examples: I listen to German podcasts.
You need to listen to local people.
context: Needing the local language while living in Germany.

my_sentence: If you go to some local hospital or school...
correction: If you go to some local hospitals or schools...
explanation: After "some," general countable places normally take plural forms. The teacher explicitly prompted "hospitals."
correct_examples: Some local hospitals provide translation.
I visited several schools.
context: Situations where local residents may not speak English.

my_sentence: Most people talk about their salary, rent, and expense.
correction: Most people talk about their salaries, rent, and expenses.
explanation: When discussing the costs and pay of many people generally, use the plural countable nouns "salaries" and "expenses." The teacher explicitly prompted "expenses."
correct_examples: People compare salaries and expenses.
Rent is one of my main expenses.
context: Topics covered in videos about the UK and Japan.

my_sentence: Maybe that's because I more care about these things.
correction: Maybe that's because I care more about these things.
explanation: With "care about," place the comparative adverb "more" after the verb: "care more about." The teacher explicitly recast the order.
correct_examples: I care more about the salary.
She cares more about safety now.
context: Explaining his interest in salaries, rent, and expenses.

my_sentence: I have experienced two or three times earthquake in Japan.
correction: I have experienced earthquakes two or three times in Japan.
explanation: Put the event noun before the frequency phrase, and use the plural "earthquakes" for repeated events. The teacher supplied the full corrected order.
correct_examples: I have experienced earthquakes twice.
She has visited Japan three times.
context: Talking about earthquakes experienced in Japan.

my_sentence: If you live some big cities...
correction: If you live in some big cities...
explanation: Use the preposition "in" before a city or group of cities. The teacher explicitly prompted it.
correct_examples: If you live in a big city, buildings are safer.
She lives in Tokyo.
context: Explaining earthquake safety in large Japanese cities.

my_sentence: You don't worry about them.
correction: You don't have to worry about them.
explanation: Use "don't have to" to express that worrying is unnecessary. The teacher explicitly supplied the modal phrase.
correct_examples: You don't have to worry about the building.
We don't have to leave yet.
context: Discussing buildings designed to withstand earthquakes.

my_sentence: I rarely see someone dying because of earthquake.
correction: I rarely see someone dying because of earthquakes.
explanation: Use the plural "earthquakes" when referring to the type of disaster generally. The teacher prompted the plural form in this discussion.
correct_examples: Few people die because of earthquakes there.
The buildings can withstand earthquakes.
context: Explaining why earthquakes in Japan seem less dangerous.
